parts.title: Transcript
parts.annotated_transcript_html:
Speaker 1: Jess
Hello, Simon. Today's lecture about Magna Carta was interesting. Did you already know about it?

Speaker 2: Simon
Only from history at school, that the bishops and barons forced King John to accept their terms in a document called Magna Carta.

Speaker 1: Jess
Yes, Latin for Great Charter. Thing is, King John didn't have much negotiating power. He was really unpopular.

Speaker 2: Simon
True, but that was because England had been in control of land in France, but the French managed to take it back.

Speaker 1: Jess
Right, and I suppose ordinary people held John responsible.

Speaker 2: Simon
Exactly. And then he wanted to raise taxes to pay for his wars. So perhaps we should be looking at Magna Carta in the context of the wars between England and France.

Speaker 1: Jess
That was what had created a dispute between the king and the ruling class in England, and the whole point of Magna Carta was to put an end to that confrontation. Arguably, there ended up being some benefits for ordinary people, but achieving those wasn't a top priority.

Speaker 2: Simon
Right. Yes, I'd done some reading before the lecture, so I wasn't surprised to hear that it was the aristocracy who had the most to gain from getting the king to agree to Magna Carta. But what I hadn't been aware of was that Magna Carta has had so much influence overseas.

Speaker 1: Jess
Especially in the US.

Speaker 2: Simon
Yeah, and the teacher said their constitution even borrows some exact wording from Magna Carta.

Speaker 1: Jess
Yeah, it's interesting that after the original Magna Carta was sealed in the year 1215, it was then tweaked and reissued in 1216, then in 1225 and 1297. Just goes to show that Magna Carta was valued, I suppose as a way of placing legal restrictions on the authority of the king, not just under King John, but during the reigns of his successors too.

Speaker 2: Simon
Yeah, John's gone down in history as being an awful king. Do you think he was just unlucky for being king at a difficult time?

Speaker 1: Jess
I don't buy that. You see, if you look at his predecessors...

Speaker 2: Simon
Other kings in the 1100s before he was crowned.

Speaker 1: Jess
Yes, Henry I, King Stephen, King Richard I. All of them had to deal with revolts or uprisings of one sort or another during their reigns. So, in a way, the opposition John encountered wasn't much different from that. So, I don't think he was particularly unlucky.
